game.opening: Queen’s Pawn Game (D02) · Chigorin setup by Black

engine.maia: Maia-1 rating-band networks 1100-1900 via zerofish WASM lc0, nodes=1

accuracy.method: mean of volatility-weighted mean and harmonic mean of per-move accuracies (Lichess formulas)

mistakes.title: The king stepped onto the promotion diagonal — and made the deflection work
mistakes.explanation: The position was already gone — Black’s h-pawn on h2, shepherded by the rook, wins against any defense — but 45.Ke3 turned a long grind into mate on the spot. The king now stands on the g1–a7 diagonal, the exact line a new queen on g1 would check along. Black’s 45…Rg1! is a pure deflection: the rook offers itself on the queening square, and 46.Rxg1 hxg1=Q+ promotes with check, so you never get a move to fight the new queen. 46% of players at your level find the right setup here — 45.Rh1, planting the rook on the queening square where nothing can budge it. You saw that idea a move too late; the deeper habit is the pre-move scan: before touching the king in a promotion race, ask which squares the new queen will check from, and stay off those lines.

takeaways.lesson: When an enemy pawn reaches the 7th (or 2nd) rank, park your rook on the queening square.
takeaways.detail: A rook sitting on the promotion square can’t be deflected by checks and never has to guess. Here 45.Rh1 holds longest; every other rook placement loses to …Rg1-style tricks. Make “rook to the queening square” your default in every pawn-down rook endgame.

takeaways.lesson: Before a king move in a promotion race, ask where the new queen will check from.
takeaways.detail: Promotion-with-check is worth a full tempo — usually the whole game. Trace the diagonals and files through the queening square (here g1–a7) and keep your king off them. It’s a three-second scan and it would have flagged 45.Ke3 instantly.

mistakes.title: The king marched forward when its job was to walk home
mistakes.explanation: After the checks 31…Bh6+ and 32…Bg7+, your king on e5 had a genuine coin-flip — and Maia’s numbers say your level treats it as one: 51% go forward with 33.Kd6, 50% turn back with 33.Kf4. The board says it isn’t close. On f4 the king shields e4 and stays within reach of g2 and h2, and after 33…Bxd4 34.Rxd4 the endgame holds (−0.4). On d6 the king has walked off the edge of its own position: 33…Ra6+! drives it further (34.Ke7 Re6+ 35.Kd7 Rxe4 simply wins the pawn), and in the game 33…Bxd4 34.Rxd4 Ra6+ 35.Kc7 Ra2 left Black’s rook to eat g2 and h2 for free while your king watched from the queenside. An advanced king is only strong when it attacks something; this one had nothing to bite and everything behind it undefended.

takeaways.lesson: Advance the king only toward something it can attack or escort; otherwise keep it in touch with its own pawns.
takeaways.detail: Before a forward king move in the endgame, name the target: a weak pawn, a passed pawn to escort, a square to invade. If you can’t name one, the move is drift. On d6 the king attacked nothing; on f4 it defended e4 and covered the kingside.

takeaways.lesson: Count the checks before stepping into open ranks and files.
takeaways.detail: Rook endgames are decided by tempo-gaining checks. Here …Ra6+ and …Re6+ were sitting on the open sixth rank, each one dragging your king further from its pawns. Run the opponent’s checks in your head first — if a check sequence wins material, the square is off-limits.

mistakes.title: One rook move gave the whole piece back
mistakes.explanation: After 24…Rc8 both of your bishops stood on the c-file in the rook’s crosshairs, and this was the move the game turned on. 25.Rd6 looks natural — it defends the c6-bishop sideways — but it commits two crimes at once: the rook occupies d6, the c5-bishop’s only retreat along the a3–f8 diagonal, and it stands on a square a bishop can hit. 25…Bf8! attacked the rook, and there was no good square: 26.Rd5 abandoned c6, and after 26…Rxc6 27.Bd4 the extra piece — and the game — was gone (+0.0). The engine’s save, 25.Ba3!, is a back-rank magic trick fewer than 1% of your peers would find (25…Rxc6 26.Rd8+ Bf8 27.Bxf8 keeps the piece). The move to remember is the one your peers actually play: 25.Bxa7 was Maia’s top choice at 14%, calmly trading the doomed c6-bishop for the a-pawn — after 25…Rxc6 you’re a clean pawn up with a passed a-pawn (+2.3) instead of a piece up, but the win is still yours to keep.

takeaways.lesson: Before defending a piece, check what square your defender takes away.
takeaways.detail: 25.Rd6 defended c6 but sat on the c5-bishop’s only escape square — one move created two trapped pieces. Whenever a defensive move lands on a diagonal or file your own piece needs, look for the opponent’s attack on the defender first. Only 7% of your level plays Rd6 here: this was a lapse, not a gap — a ten-second “what does this square cost me?” check catches it.

takeaways.lesson: When you’re a piece up and a tactic threatens to win it back, give back the smallest unit and simplify.
takeaways.detail: You didn’t need to save both bishops — you needed to stay winning. 25.Bxa7 banks a pawn, concedes the skewered bishop, and leaves a trivially better endgame. Up material, always price the “pay the tax and simplify” option before hunting for the move that keeps everything.

mistakes.title: The center push released the pressure — the capture won a pawn
mistakes.explanation: When Black struck with 11…e5, your last move 11.Bxf3 had quietly loaded the answer: 12.dxe5! wins a pawn outright, because after 12…Nxe5 the f3-bishop’s long diagonal opens onto b7 — 13.Bxb7 picks it off, and 13…Qxd1 14.Rxd1 changes nothing (+0.9 and a clean extra pawn). Instead 12.d5 gained a tempo on the knight but closed the very diagonal that was your best asset, and after 12…Ne7 13.c4 c6! Black dissolved the pawn spearhead and full equality set in. This one is a pattern worth drilling, because your level splits three ways here — 27% trade on c6, 26% push d5, and only 16% find the capture: when your opponent challenges a center pawn, calculate the capture first, especially when taking opens a line for a piece you just developed.

takeaways.lesson: When a center pawn is challenged, calculate the capture before the push.
takeaways.detail: Pushing feels safe because it keeps the pawn; but the capture is the move that opens lines, and here it won material because b7 hung at the end of the f3-bishop’s diagonal. Make “what do I get if I just take?” the first branch you look at, every time the tension breaks.

takeaways.lesson: Track which diagonals and files your last few moves opened — then use them.
takeaways.detail: 11.Bxf3 put a bishop on the h1–a8 diagonal one move before this decision, and the win of the b7 pawn lived on exactly that line. After every capture or pawn move, update your mental list of newly open lines; tactics almost always arrive on the line that opened last.

drillLinks.label: When to push the pawns in front of the king — discussion

highlights.note: Castled by move 6, before the centre opened — fewer than 1% of your band gets the king safe this early in this structure. Your 87%-accuracy game grew out of a textbook setup.
When Maia tried the …e4 trick you calmly took with the d5-bishop — the one clean way through the small tactics, staying a full piece up at +5.

openingReport.note: Your 3.e3 left book quietly — solid, but 3.c4 or 3.Bf4 are the moves that press against Maia's Chigorin-style …Nc6 setup. The first improvable decision came much later (8.e4, 53.9% → 46.5%); the opening itself was your cleanest yet. Third analyzed game with 1.d4 d5 — worth 20 minutes of study.
